content.text: The cloud service model — IaaS, PaaS, or SaaS — directly determines which security layers the customer must operate and which Microsoft manages. Understanding this boundary per model is critical for designing secure Azure architectures: moving from IaaS to PaaS does not make a system secure automatically; it shifts which controls the customer must configure, not whether the customer has obligations.

attrs.alt: Customer vs Microsoft responsibility scope by service model

content.text: IaaS: Full Stack Customer Responsibility Above the Hypervisor

Infrastructure as a Service gives customers raw compute, storage, and networking. Microsoft secures the physical datacenter, hardware, and the hypervisor that isolates VMs. Everything above the hypervisor is the customer's responsibility: guest OS installation and patching, application runtime and libraries, application code, network security group rules, disk encryption, antivirus and endpoint detection, backup, and identity configuration on the VM. IaaS has the largest customer security footprint of the three models.
Nothing — customer configures VNet overlay only
Install updates, set patch mode, configure SELinux/AppArmor or Windows Defender
NSG rules, route tables, Azure Firewall, NIC-level config
Install, update, and harden Node.js/Java/.NET/Python runtimes and libraries
Fix vulnerabilities, use parameterized queries, input validation
SSH key auth (no password), Entra ID SSH login, PIM for admin accounts
Azure Disk Encryption (BitLocker/DM-Crypt) or SSE with CMK
Configure Azure Backup policy, test restore procedures

# IaaS: Azure Virtual Machine — customer owns OS and everything above

# 1. OS patching: set automatic patching (platform patches, customer controls window)
az vm update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --set osProfile.linuxConfiguration.patchSettings.patchMode=AutomaticByPlatform \
        osProfile.linuxConfiguration.patchSettings.assessmentMode=AutomaticByPlatform

# 2. OS disk encryption (customer must enable — not automatic)
az vm encryption enable \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --disk-encryption-keyvault myKeyVault

# 3. Network: customer must restrict inbound access via NSG
az network nsg rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --nsg-name myNSG \
  --name DenyAllInbound \
  --priority 4096 \
  --source-address-prefixes '*' \
  --destination-port-ranges '*' \
  --access Deny

# 4. Enable Azure Monitor Agent for guest-level monitoring (customer must install)
az vm extension set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vm-name myVM \
  --name AzureMonitorLinuxAgent \
  --publisher Microsoft.Azure.Monitor \
  --version 1.0

# 5. Enable Defender for Endpoint (EDR — customer must enable per VM)
az security pricing create \
  --name VirtualMachines \
  --tier Standard
PaaS: OS and Runtime Managed; Application and Data Remain Customer

Platform as a Service removes the OS and runtime management burden from the customer. App Service, Azure SQL, Azure Functions, Cosmos DB, and Azure Kubernetes Service (managed control plane) all fall in this category. Microsoft patches the underlying VM fleet, OS, database engine, and application runtime. However, the customer must still configure network access (firewall rules, private endpoints), authentication settings (disable weak auth, enforce managed identity), data encryption choices (PMK vs CMK), and application security (the code running on the platform is always the customer's responsibility).

The most common PaaS misconfiguration pattern is treating 'Microsoft manages the OS' as equivalent to 'this service is secure.' A PaaS service with a public endpoint, no firewall rules, and SQL injection vulnerabilities in the application layer is not secure regardless of who patches the OS.
Nothing (but customer controls restart windows for App Service)
Choose runtime version; update app to compatible versions when old runtimes are deprecated
Configure firewall rules, private endpoints, VNet integration (Microsoft provides the controls; customer must configure them)
Enable managed identity; disable access keys where possible; configure Conditional Access
PMK is default; configure CMK in Key Vault if required by policy/regulation
Input validation, secure coding, dependency updates, no hardcoded credentials
Enable audit logging, diagnostic settings, threat protection per service

# PaaS: App Service and Azure SQL — OS/runtime managed by Microsoft
# Customer responsibilities: network config, auth settings, code security, data

# App Service (PaaS): Microsoft patches the underlying OS and runtime
# Customer must still:

# 1. Enforce HTTPS only (customer configures, not auto)
az webapp update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp \
  --https-only true

# 2. Set minimum TLS version
az webapp config set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp \
  --min-tls-version 1.2

# 3. Disable public network access (use private endpoint instead)
az webapp update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp \
  --set publicNetworkAccess=Disabled

# 4. Enable managed identity (eliminates secrets in app config)
az webapp identity assign \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp

# Azure SQL (PaaS): Microsoft patches SQL Server engine
# Customer must:

# 5. Enable Advanced Threat Protection (detect SQL injection attempts)
az sql server threat-policy update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mySQLServer \
  --state Enabled

# 6. Disable public endpoint (use Private Link)
az sql server update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySQLServer \
  --restrict-outbound-network-access true

az sql server firewall-rule delete \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mySQLServer \
  --name AllowAllWindowsAzureIps
SaaS: Application Managed; Identity and Data Remain Customer

Software as a Service (Microsoft 365, Dynamics 365, Power Platform, Entra ID as a SaaS identity provider) shifts the largest portion of responsibility to Microsoft. Microsoft manages infrastructure, OS, runtime, middleware, and the application itself. The customer's primary security obligations narrow to identity governance (user accounts, MFA policies, Conditional Access), data governance (classifying content, configuring DLP policies, managing data lifecycle), and application-level configuration (which external domains are trusted, what sharing permissions are allowed).

Even in SaaS, the customer is responsible for regulatory compliance of their data. Using Microsoft 365 for healthcare does not make the organization HIPAA-compliant. The organization must sign a BAA with Microsoft, configure audit logging, and implement access controls for protected health information — Microsoft provides the tools and certifications; the customer must configure and operate them.

# SaaS example: Microsoft 365 — customer owns identity, data, and app configuration
# (Microsoft Graph API for tenant configuration)

# Customer must enforce MFA for all users (not automatic in SaaS)
# Conditional Access policy via Microsoft Graph (PowerShell example)
#   New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy -BodyParameter @{
#     displayName = 'Require MFA for all users'
#     state = 'enabled'
#     conditions = @{ users = @{ includeUsers = @('All') }; applications = @{ includeApplications = @('All') } }
#     grantControls = @{ operator = 'OR'; builtInControls = @('mfa') }
#   }

# Azure AD (Entra ID) — SaaS used for identity; customer manages users and policies

# Audit sign-ins for risky users (customer must monitor — not auto-remediated)
az ad user list \
  --filter "accountEnabled eq true" \
  --query "[].{upn:userPrincipalName, id:id}" \
  --output table

# In SaaS, Microsoft handles infrastructure compliance certifications
# but customer must configure workload compliance:

# Check Entra ID audit logs for user and admin activity
az monitor activity-log list \
  --caller 'admin@contoso.com' \
  --start-time '2026-06-01T00:00:00Z' \
  --query "[].{operation:operationName.localizedValue, status:properties.statusCode, time:eventTimestamp}" \
  --output table

# Enable Entra ID Identity Protection (risk-based Conditional Access)
# (configured in Entra ID portal — requires P2 license)

Network controls shift from fully customer-owned (IaaS) to shared configuration (PaaS) to primarily Microsoft-managed (SaaS). In IaaS, the customer creates and configures the VNet, subnets, NSGs, and route tables from scratch. In PaaS, Microsoft provides the underlying network infrastructure but the customer must explicitly configure VNet integration (outbound) and Private Endpoints (inbound) to isolate services from the public internet. In SaaS, Microsoft manages the network, but customers configure which IP ranges or trusted locations can access the service via Conditional Access policies.

# Network controls: customer responsibility in IaaS; shared in PaaS; Microsoft in SaaS

# IaaS: customer fully controls network topology
# Create a VNet with separate subnets for tiers
az network vnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVNet \
  --address-prefix 10.0.0.0/16

az network vnet subnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --name WebSubnet \
  --address-prefix 10.0.1.0/24

az network vnet subnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --name DataSubnet \
  --address-prefix 10.0.2.0/24

# PaaS: customer configures VNet integration for outbound and Private Endpoint for inbound
# App Service outbound VNet integration
az webapp vnet-integration add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp \
  --vnet myVNet \
  --subnet WebSubnet

# Azure SQL inbound Private Endpoint (replaces public endpoint)
az network private-endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name sqlPrivateEndpoint \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet DataSubnet \
  --private-connection-resource-id $(az sql server show -g myRG -n mySQLServer --query id -o tsv) \
  --group-id sqlServer \
  --connection-name sql-connection

# Create Private DNS zone for SQL private endpoint resolution
az network private-dns zone create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name 'privatelink.database.windows.net'

az network private-dns link vnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --zone-name 'privatelink.database.windows.net' \
  --name sqlDNSLink \
  --virtual-network myVNet \
  --registration-enabled false
NSG outbound rules + UDR + Azure Firewall
VNet integration + UDR for forced tunnel
Azure DDoS Standard on VNet (customer enables)
Inherited from platform; DDoS Standard on VNet
VNet by default; customer configures peering/VPN
ExpressRoute / VPN for on-premises access to SaaS
Customer configures private DNS or custom DNS server
Private DNS zone for private endpoints (customer)

Identity is the one area where the customer bears full responsibility in all three service models. In IaaS, this means configuring authentication to the guest OS (SSH key-based auth, disabling password auth, enabling Entra ID-based SSH login for Linux). In PaaS, it means assigning managed identities to compute resources and granting them least-privilege RBAC access to downstream services instead of storing connection strings in application configuration. In SaaS, it means enforcing Conditional Access policies (requiring MFA, blocking risky sign-ins, controlling which devices can access the service).

# Identity responsibilities remain with the customer across all service models

# IaaS: configure Entra ID-based SSH login for Linux VMs
az vm extension set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vm-name myLinuxVM \
  --name AADSSHLoginForLinux \
  --publisher Microsoft.Azure.ActiveDirectory

# Grant VM Login User role (non-admin SSH via Entra ID)
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Virtual Machine User Login' \
  --assignee developer@contoso.com \
  --scope $(az vm show -g myRG -n myLinuxVM --query id -o tsv)

# PaaS: assign managed identity to App Service (no connection string needed)
az webapp identity assign \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp

# Grant App Service identity access to Key Vault secrets
APP_IDENTITY=$(az webapp identity show -g myRG -n myWebApp --query principalId -o tsv)
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
  --assignee $APP_IDENTITY \
  --scope $(az keyvault show -g myRG -n myKeyVault --query id -o tsv)

# PaaS: grant App Service identity access to Azure SQL (AAD auth, no password)
# Run in Azure SQL:
# CREATE USER [myWebApp] FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER;
# ALTER ROLE db_datareader ADD MEMBER [myWebApp];
# ALTER ROLE db_datawriter ADD MEMBER [myWebApp];

# SaaS: configure Conditional Access — require MFA from all locations for all apps
# (done via Entra ID portal or Microsoft Graph API — requires Entra ID P1 or P2)

# Check for users not covered by MFA Conditional Access
az ad user list \
  --filter "userType eq 'Member'" \
  --query "length(@)" \
  --output tsv

Compliance is always the customer's workload-level obligation. Microsoft's compliance certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2, PCI DSS, HIPAA BAA) cover the Azure platform infrastructure — the physical facilities, hypervisor, and managed services. They do not cover the customer's workload configuration, application code, or data handling practices. The customer must independently verify and document their own controls. In practical terms, this means enabling audit logging, configuring data retention, enforcing encryption at rest and in transit, and generating compliance evidence for auditors.

# Compliance obligations by service model

# IaaS: customer must configure audit logging (not automatic)
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --resource $(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query id -o tsv) \
  --name vm-audit-logs \
  --workspace $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myWorkspace --query id -o tsv) \
  --logs '[{"category":"Administrative","enabled":true}]'

# PaaS: enable Azure SQL auditing (writes to Log Analytics)
az sql server audit-policy update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySQLServer \
  --state Enabled \
  --log-analytics-target-state Enabled \
  --log-analytics-workspace-resource-id \
  $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myWorkspace --query id -o tsv) \
  --retention-days 90

# PaaS: enable App Service access logs (who called which endpoint, when)
az webapp log config \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp \
  --web-server-logging filesystem \
  --access-log-format combined

# Check Defender for Cloud regulatory compliance status
az security regulatory-compliance-standards list \
  --query "[].{standard:name, passed:properties.passedControls, failed:properties.failedControls, unsupported:properties.unsupportedControls}" \
  --output table

# Azure Policy: assign PCI DSS compliance initiative
az policy assignment create \
  --name pci-dss-compliance \
  --display-name 'PCI DSS v4 Compliance' \
  --policy-set-definition '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/496eeda9-8f2f-4d5e-8dfd-204f33a7f8fe' \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>

requires the most customer effort. The customer must install and configure logging agents (Azure Monitor Agent), set up log retention policies in Log Analytics, enable endpoint detection, and document all OS-level hardening steps for auditors.

Microsoft provides built-in audit logging capabilities (Azure SQL audit, App Service access logs, Storage diagnostic settings), but the customer must explicitly enable them and configure retention periods. Many PaaS services have audit logging disabled by default.

the compliance boundary is clearest in SaaS — Microsoft provides a compliance manager that shows exactly which controls Microsoft owns vs. which the customer must implement. For Microsoft 365, customer controls include DLP policies, sensitivity labels, and access reviews.
Mixed Models: AKS as an Example

Some Azure services span multiple responsibility tiers. Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) is a useful example: Microsoft manages the Kubernetes control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager) as a PaaS component at no additional cost. The customer is responsible for the worker node VMs (OS patching through the node upgrade channel), container images (vulnerability scanning), Kubernetes RBAC policies, network policies (Calico or Azure NPM), and pod security configurations. This makes AKS a split responsibility model within a single service.

# AKS (mixed IaaS/PaaS): control plane is managed by Microsoft; nodes are customer

# Microsoft manages: Kubernetes API server, etcd, controller manager, scheduler
# Customer manages: node pool VMs, container images, RBAC policies, network policies

# 1. Enable Microsoft Defender for Containers (detects runtime threats in pods)
az security pricing create \
  --name Containers \
  --tier Standard

# 2. Enable Azure Policy add-on (enforce pod security standards)
az aks enable-addons \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKS \
  --addons azure-policy

# 3. Apply built-in pod security policy: deny privileged containers
az policy assignment create \
  --name deny-privileged-pods \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/95edb821-ddaf-4404-9732-666045e056b4' \
  --scope $(az aks show -g myRG -n myAKS --query id -o tsv)

# 4. Enable RBAC and Azure AD integration (customer must enable — not default on older clusters)
az aks update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKS \
  --enable-aad \
  --aad-admin-group-object-ids <entra-group-id>

# 5. Enable node auto-upgrade (customer decides channel; OS patches are customer-controlled)
az aks update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKS \
  --auto-upgrade-channel patch \
  --node-os-upgrade-channel NodeImage
Operational Cost as a Responsibility Dimension

The service model also determines who is responsible for cost optimization. In IaaS, the customer pays for idle compute — a stopped (but not deallocated) VM still incurs compute charges. In PaaS, Microsoft handles infrastructure provisioning but the customer configures autoscaling and tier selection. In SaaS, cost is subscription-based and the customer manages license assignment and usage. In each model, right-sizing (choosing the correct VM size, tier, or license count) is always the customer's responsibility, not Microsoft's.

# Cost and responsibility: service model also affects operational cost model

# IaaS VM costs: customer pays for compute even when idle (unless deallocated)
# Auto-shutdown for dev VMs (not available in production without workaround)
az vm auto-shutdown \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDevVM \
  --time 1900 \
  --timezone 'UTC'

# Right-sizing: customer responsible for choosing correct VM size
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource $(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query id -o tsv) \
  --metric 'Percentage CPU' 'Available Memory Bytes' \
  --interval PT1H \
  --start-time '2026-06-23T00:00:00Z' \
  --end-time '2026-06-30T00:00:00Z' \
  --aggregation Average \
  --output table

# PaaS: App Service autoscale (customer configures; Microsoft scales the infrastructure)
az monitor autoscale create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --resource $(az appservice plan show -g myRG -n myASP --query id -o tsv) \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Web/serverfarms \
  --name asp-autoscale \
  --min-count 2 \
  --max-count 10 \
  --count 2

az monitor autoscale rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --autoscale-name asp-autoscale \
  --scale out 2 \
  --condition 'CpuPercentage > 75 avg 5m'

# PaaS: Azure SQL cost — serverless (auto-pause when idle, billing pauses)
az sql db update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mySQLServer \
  --name myDB \
  --edition GeneralPurpose \
  --compute-model Serverless \
  --auto-pause-delay 60

A core principle of shared responsibility: responsibility does not equal blame for incidents. Microsoft's responsibility means they provide a secure platform. Customer responsibility means the customer must configure and operate their portion correctly. A breach resulting from a customer misconfiguration (open RDP, default password, public storage bucket) is the customer's security failure even if Azure's underlying platform was secure.

The three service models distribute security responsibility along a spectrum. IaaS gives the customer maximum control and maximum responsibility: OS patching, network rules, disk encryption, runtime security, application code, backup, and identity all require explicit customer action. PaaS removes OS and runtime management but the customer must still configure network access controls (private endpoints, firewall rules), authentication settings, data encryption choices, and application code security. SaaS narrows customer obligations to identity governance (MFA, Conditional Access, user provisioning), data governance (classification, DLP, retention), and application configuration. Across all three models, identity and data are permanently the customer's responsibility — a compromised admin account or unclassified sensitive data is a customer failure regardless of the service model. Moving up the stack (IaaS to PaaS to SaaS) reduces operational burden but does not eliminate the customer's security obligations; it shifts which controls the customer must configure.
